content.full_text:
State of California

Memorandum

To    :    Members of the Commission                    Date  :  March 12, 1976


From  :  FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
         Natalie West

Subject:  Section 89001

Section 89001 provides:

     No legislative newsletter or other mass mailing shall be sent
     at public expense by or on behalf of any elected state officer
     after the elected state officer has filed a declaration of
     candidacy for any office.

The term "mass mailing" is defined in Section 82041.5 to mean two
hundred or more identical or nearly identical pieces of mail but
does not include a form letter or other mail which is sent in
response to a request, letter or other inquiry.  Members of the
legislature have asked questions concerning the applicability of
Section 89001 to proposed mailings, including those described
below.  Each question refers to a mailing of 200 or more pieces,
which a legislator would like to send after filing his declaration
of candidacy.

1.   Information regarding pending legislation.

     a.   May a legislator mail information concerning committee
          hearing dates on proposed legislation?

     b.   May a legislator mail information about pending
          legislation to special interest groups?

          1)   Is the conclusion the same whether the recipient
               groups are all in the legislator's district or are
               located throughout the state?

          2)   Is the conclusion the same if the legislator mails
               information about legislation that he has
               introduced?

2.   Information regarding enacted legislation.

     a.   May a legislator send a newsletter to members of a
          profession describing recently enacted legislation that
          affects the members of that profession?

     b.   May a legislator send a description of legislation he's
          supported to a "VIP" mailing list consisting of 400
          names?


Members of the Commission                -2-                March 12, 1976


     c.    May a legislator send out a newsletter with a coupon that
           can be mailed in to obtain additional information on
           specific topics? If such a newsletter is mailed before
           filing the declaration of candidacy, may he fill the
           coupon requests?

3.   Press releases.

     a.    May a legislator send press releases to the media?

     b.    May a legislator send copies of his press releases to
           interested constituents?

4.   Questionnaires.

     a.    May a legislator send a questionnaire to his
           constituents?

     b.    May a legislator distribute the results of a
           questionnaire to his constituents, if the questionnaire
           was distributed before filing the declaration of
           candidacy?

5.   Miscellaneous.

     a.    May the legislator mail notices of a meeting at which he
           will speak?

     b.    May the legislator mail congratulatory letters to new
           citizens, high school graduates, etc.?

The Commission must determine which, if any, of the above mailings
may be sent at public expense after a legislator has filed a
declaration of candidacy. In doing so, the Commission should
interpret Section 89001 to further the purpose set forth in Section
81002(f), which states that:

     laws and practices unfairly favoring incumbents should be
     abolished in order that elections may be conducted more
     fairly.

On the other hand the Commission may wish to acknowledge certain
countervailing considerations such as maintaining a well informed
electorate.

The problem is a thorny one and there is some disagreement among
the staff concerning the issues raised by Section 89001. We have
developed the following criteria to answer the questions posed
above:

1.   A press release sent only to the press is not prohibited.

2.   A mailing concerning the activities of a particular
     legislative committee may be sent by the committee; not by the
     chairman or member.


I apologize, but I cannot provide an accurate transcription of this document due to the illegibility of the source material. A clearer or higher-contrast version of this image would be needed to transcribe the content accurately.

Members of the Commission                -3-                March 12, 1976


3.     Mailings sent to special interest groups or certain lists are
not permissable.

These three criteria are tentative, at best, but may provide a
starting place for discussion.
